title: AI Unicorns Don’t Exist | The Aboard Podcast 2026-07-14

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hi, I'm Paul Ford.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm Rich Ziade.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is the Aboard podcast, the podcast about how AI is changing the world of software and it really is. Rich, I want to talk to you about something you wrote.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Did you enjoy it?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We'll get to that in a minute.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But in the meantime we'll play the theme song and then we'll tell people what Aboard is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Play it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let me take a pass.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Aboard is a strategic AI consultant firm. But you know what's different?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We consult by delivering. We deliver software. So we do the thinking, we help you make sense of all the change that's coming into the world. But then we actually make something to prove out how things are supposed to be working.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Demos?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No. No, no, no, no, no, no. I don't know.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The real deal.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no. The real deal. Real software. That's the difference. And we have a whole process that we're building, we're building tools that help us do it. And we can help you out too. So I think get in touch if you want to learn more, hello@aboard.com. That concludes our marketing section. But it's what we work on every day. It is actually, it's serious to us because we want to build better tools and we want to people, we want to help people succeed with this new stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And that brings me to why I've called you in here today.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Uh oh.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So every now and then you write the newsletter.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And it's very funny because I used to be a journalist and so people kind of take me for granted. But then they're like, 'That Rich, he's very thoughtful.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, nobody who likes my writing talks like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: He made an amazing, it was a, he put marshmallows and jam in a, and he made amazing, what is that salad that they have in the Midwest? Do you know what I'm talking about? They put marshmallows in things and it's got like Jell-O. Ambrosia! You ever had Ambrosia in a Jell-O mold?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They don't have that in Lebanon.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Bay Ridge has never seen Ambrosia. Anyway, go Google Ambrosia.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes, I write for the newsletter sometimes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You do. And actually what happens is people always like it because you come in and advise CEOs. You can't help it. It's what you do. You get up in the morning and you start advising CEOs like in the shower.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yes, I do. And I gotta tell you, for those that don't know, Paul Ford is a very well-known, accomplished writer. And I am the John Oates of the media arm of Aboard.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We actually have a very good intake, good give and take on this. We don't even have to play out that aspect of our relationship. Actually, I want to talk about your piece. I always like when you write, and actually everybody does. Like everybody's always like, 'Oh, yeah.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I do appreciate that, and it does mean a lot to let a little light into the shadow.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There we are. That's, that's what we're talking about. No, no, but you tend to come in with...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Actionable business advice.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm very direct.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Now here is what's going on with... thank you, thank you Paul. So here's the piece, it's from June 24th. I mean to be clear, I always edit them. Anyway, 'Maybe the valley could listen a little' is the title of the piece and the subtitle is 'Tech's disconnect with the real world is a feature, not a bug, but when it comes to AI that's a big problem.' Now I want to call us out on something here because I think it's important. We've been talking about AI and software for more than a year now.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: More than a year.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Way more than a year, as we've watched our industry evolve. And I would say about really around six months ago, Claude Code came out and I was like 'Whoa, what's happening?' and that is not a surprise to anybody at this point, everybody has now had a 'Whoa, what's happening?'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And at that point, it really looked like we were at an inflection point with the industry. And you always advised caution. I'm going to give you a lot of credit there. Like even when I was like 'Dude, I've never seen anything like this before,' you're like 'Hold on a minute, humans are involved.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I think explain a little bit. I came in and I showed you something that you'd never seen before. I showed you that you could create relatively good code out of prompt even as a like even without really kind of programming it and you could create something that used to take months to create. And you didn't disagree at all that I had done that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no, no.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so why did you not—I saw that as hugely disruptive. Why didn't you see it that way?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Two reasons, really. One reason is the ability to hack on your own without talking to anyone is wonderful.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is wonderful. I'm a hacker too. I like to hack, I've done that alone and it's the sense of control for someone like me who is often asking other people for things is... and I love technology, it's fun, really fun.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We have a joke about this. Whenever we run into like a CTO, they always end up talking about their home stereo system for like two hours.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Cause it's the one thing they can control. They love technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But they can't control technologists.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They can't control technologists.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So they get into like Dolby surround sound.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and so I think, I think the invention that's landed—and we don't have to go all the way back to AI, which has been cooking for years—but let's focus on making apps or generating code with AI. Pretty much led by Claude Code and there's Codex and there's some other tools. It's very much a personal productivity tool. It is a tool that is landed in engineers' laps and they can do stuff. And if you're a creative thinker like yourself, you're unusual. You're technical, but you're also kind of a meandering thinker, and so you apply a lot of like things that are coming in from outside of tech to what you build. And that's unusual. I'm unusual because I'm a deeply, deeply sympathetic product person.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: thinker who wants products to be used, but I'm also technical to an extent.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I personally always love when when business leaders are trying to be nice to me and they say I'm non-orthogonal. That's always a really good one.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Is that really?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, that happens.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Anyway, let me finish your thought, but then I want to bring it back. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So that's great and it's a great tool, right? And and then the shift is okay, I can make software now, right? And and so...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Look, there's millions of new apps that are dropping in app stores and whatnot.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This is real, right? So App Store submissions are up.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're up. Because you can do it on your at your on your own over a weekend and then you don't know how to apply for the Apple App Store and you could use AI to do that process. So it's all it's all been taken care of.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I mean I think a really good we've now asked a lot of people including ourselves like, hey, have you seen an AI-coded app that's transformative? One that you want to pay for? One that you're like, okay. Because what happens is the valley gets in there and they're like, look at this. Anyone can make software. The days of a one-person unicorn are here. We're going to have Rich is going to go off and make a piece of software worth a billion dollars and that's like the most exciting thing in the world to us. But why isn't there more software coming out where people are like, oh thank God, we solved it?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'll tell you why. Because there are very, very few, if any, if any human unicorns. There are people that are good at design and dabble in tech. There are people that are great at tech and great engineers that dabble with design. There are people who make very specific nudgy tools for something they're a fan of that no one else cares about. There are product managers who don't care for any of the details. And and...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm going to nail it down for you though because there's lots of people who can do lots of different things. Product is actually miserable work. It's not about a joyful expression where you finally get all your ideas together, turn them into software, you put them on the App Store and everybody's happy with you. Product is kind of a grind. And so the person who can take something joyful and exciting that they love about the tech industry and motivate themselves to learn and do all the different crafts at once, who can also turn it into something long-term, large and viable. Literally there're very few examples. I'll give you one example, it's a weird one. Stardew Valley. Okay. A mostly one-person developed game that took years and years. Isolated. And worked on it and worked on it and just an absolutely vast hit. But boy, after that, like it's a real short list of people who can live inside that world long enough to make something transformative.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I think people are getting dopamine hits by venturing out into other skills and other crafts that aren't their core. So designers are feeling really empowered because there's Figma Make and they can build libraries and they can build WordPress plugins and that feels good because they used to have to ask for it and that feels...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: was good, right? And then there are engineers who are like, wow, I could skip the design phase, right? And I can go do that, right? And so I think the really the real tell here though is that the best I've been making, I've led teams that build software for 30 plus years, the best work we ever put out was when we assembled a real interdisciplinary all-star team. Always. Always. And that's normal. I'm not saying anything shocking.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let me describe the pro- because I'm doing way more vibe coding than you are.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You are doing some.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let me describe what happens. So I've built a couple relatively good small products around things like CRM, some enhanced text search kind of stuff and so on. And I've turned to you, you will acknowledge to the audience, I've turned to you and I've said, we need to get to a place where I can bring you in as product manager to get this out of my hands.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I keep screwing around in the meantime because I'm trying to learn.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you're also enjoying yourself.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: A little bit. So here's what happens. I realize something is missing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then I'm like, I tell I prompt it into being.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: A new feature, a new add-on, a new way to look a visualization.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then that visualization, which is not the main core of what I'm trying to do, it's a distraction.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Is just not quite where I need it to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't need it at all. No one needs it. I'm the only person using the software right now. But now you know what has become the only and most important thing in my entire life?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Finishing that visualization.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then while I'm working on that visualization, I realize that I could abstract it into a library so I could reuse it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then you know what becomes the most important thing in my life?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Abstracting it into a library.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Building a library. And then, and then each component of the library and so, and because this enables it, the minute somebody starts saying, you know, you can parallelize this with lots of agents, you know you're in a pickle.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because that means more software, whereas absolutely no one outside of our world is actually sitting there saying, I need more software.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No one has ever said those words.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No. Let's go back to the valley.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Go back. Okay, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, that's what your piece is about.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The piece is about that. And what the piece is about is a very particular kind of gaslighting, which is, listen, do not hold your core skills as precious. These these are immense powers we are handing you now.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Disruption time!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Do not do not ask for permission anymore. Mm. Do not wait for that sign off from another group anymore. Just go do it. And if you don't do it, if you don't do it, you will be eaten alive. There is always an insinuation of extinction from the valley, always. It's always been there. It's a way to create anxiety and paranoia.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I can actually be more sympathetic than that. It's not a very big city. It's it's actually nowhere near as big as New York, San Francisco and the Bay. There's it's like we're like 30 million, they're like 8 all in. Okay? It's a monoculture.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And when you are in a monoculture, when you really believe in something and everyone around you believes it and it's the only...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: thing they're talking about, it starts to feel very existential as to how it's going to work out or not. It has to work. And so they did this with Blockbuster—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, but that's not what I mean by existential. What I mean by existential is you could order a car on your phone, a cab. They immediately saw car service with a, you know, dispatcher and taxis as quaint. Like it was the end. They were telling everyone, you can do this now the easy way or you can do it the hard way, but we're coming for you. And the truth is sometimes they do come for you and it wins a lot of the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, that's what I'm saying though, they don't understand that there's a whole another culture way like for them, it's not just that Uber like should get in there and it's actually like a moral imperative. It becomes this thing where it's like unless that happens and it happens to be that the moral imperatives align with what will make them the most money. And so that culture just metabolizes and moves so quickly because there's nothing that competes with it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right, that's right. And here's the fail here. The fail here is—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Here meaning AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. And the truth is there is immense value in certain particular use cases. But when you're telling someone that the idea of building successful, usable software is now in everyone's hands is completely underestimating the fact that two things. One, software is immensely complex. It just is. If you go into businesses, there's a million edge cases and whatnot. But there's another thing that they overlook, which is that a software artifact is actually a cultural artifact and a social artifact that has to be adopted. If it's not adopted, it doesn't mean anything. And unless you're making something for yourself. You want to manage your own stamp collection, God bless. But the truth is these things require engagement and they require a certain amount of humility to make successful. Almost always. Almost always. The amount of patience that Apple uses before they decide that a feature could go in speaks to not them being lazy. It speaks to them being incredibly cautious about making sure there's engagement. And they even fail sometimes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, but this is why I find I think in some ways Apple can be very baffling to the rest of the valley because they seem slow.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Absolutely.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But that is I think you're right and I think kind of from the very beginning they've been very aware. This was Steve Jobs Apple II. We are going to design this thing. And that— I don't know if people can understand how bizarre of an idea that was in the 70s.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It was the nerdiest computer meetups, people trading circuit boards. Design is— that's like saying we're going to start to learn how to dance.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think he went to Frog Design for that or for the Mac—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I can't remember but he went to like a big design shop.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: He cared about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: He cared about it, and he emphasized it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And he spent lots of money to make it look sleek and nice to the touch because he wanted to land it in culture. Which, to be clear, this is not him being like I want to create the next great play or poem, it's him saying I want to sell this at Sears. Like I want to sell this...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I want this in your den.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I want it to look like it lives next to a stereo.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Right? And that, you know, his big... one of his big design influences was Braun, B-R-A-U-N, because you know and if you look at early Apple design and Braun design you can really see a lot of...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I own an electric shaver by Braun.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Do you electric shave?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, I do a little polish at the end.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, that's good. I never knew that about you and it's been over 200 years.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: What? That I do that?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well I know you shave. Not for this podcast, but yeah. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay. So...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Alright, so let me...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So, but let me keep going.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh. Don't stop.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It actually gets worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The world of productivity software or business software or software you use at work is far, far bigger than consumer software. Like financially it's bigger, people have to use it because you have to process payroll or you have to...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's not just that, but enormous numbers of careers are downstream of it. What is it like two-thirds? If you're going to spend money on enterprise software, like you spend a dollar you're going to spend two dollars kind of supporting and maintaining and installing it...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The people around it and the tools, yeah. So I gave issue one, which is building great software alone is really hard because there's a lot of different skills and disciplines that are required and very few people have them all.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean the way I'm expressing this is that code got a lot easier but product did not.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Bingo. Right? But there's another problem. Especially because the AI companies because they're under enormous pressure to meet the value expectations that they've put out in the world, they know they have to go to the next frontier, which is business software. They have to. Because there's just not enough money.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So here's what's happened. The company Palantir, which is very AI adjacent...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Adorable company.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh boy. Let's not talk about... Let's just leave it real high level.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Cuties. I'm pro-Palantir.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Can I say that?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Why not?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We'll talk about this some other time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're in hospitals, dude.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't want to talk about Palantir anymore right now. We've probably talked about it in the past.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Go Palantir.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Stop it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They make an amazing electric razor by the...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I am done with this conversation. No, so they're very AI adjacent and...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They have this concept of a thing called the forward deployed engineer.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, which is a little military, but the idea is it's like kind of someone... it's essentially a consultant.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. A forward deployed engineer is a very fancy, expensive way to say something already pretty fancy and expensive: consultant. And what does that consultant do? They come in and they help you use these tools, especially Palantir's tools, with a lot of AI to transform, you know, the Cleveland Police Department.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'll do you one better. Do you know what a forward deployed engineer...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't. They're a diplomat.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay. Before they can parachute in Palantir's tools, they are going into some of the most grizzly organizations that have some of the most mundane, archaic, overly convoluted processes to get anything done.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Often governments, so it's intensely regulated, etcetera, etcetera.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hospitals, government, massive non-profits. And you know what they say? You know what they're trained to say, these forward deployed engineers as you like to call them?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I love you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's really interesting, the way you guys work that way. I think that's really cool. Oh, so I was wrong. Okay. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's really, really, really interesting, and we're going to make sure we capture that. Well, no, you know there's another thing they say is— 'Hey, that was, it's too bad about what happened with Deloitte over the last 18 years.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But we're going to get you something useful in about four months.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And that feels— that is not to you and me a miracle. That's a miracle to some, to the largest civic org or the hospital system or whatever. They're like, 'What?'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And so I'm going to get to the problem, okay, that the valley is about to slam up against, which is they have captured mass scale on a consumer level, right? And they need to somehow now—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Billions. Billions of people.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Billions of people, and they need to somehow now translate that currency into business currency. And that's really hard to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, 'cause they literally, they've run out of human revenue.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I want to use two words that we were saying a lot of, and we felt like we had to, that I'm hearing less of. Agentic enterprise.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, there's still a lot of agent talk out there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There's a lot of agent talk out there. I'm even hearing about agents that we— agents are going to be so useful, they're going to talk to other agents, so they'll meet in an alleyway and they'll do the deal there. And this way there's no humans at all.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm, I'm hearing about that too. So there is, but why are you saying that that's less important?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think, I think the valley is taking swings. Do you know why? They have to because org charts and organizations and the way shit gets done today is impenetrable. And you, you think you're going to change it, and you get to chip away bit by bit because it is humbling to go into large organizations that have deeply calcified cultures and drop things in. It doesn't work. It simply does not work. We have to show that patience. We may spend months before we can articulate a solution, right? And that's a reality that is outside of the unicorn and building great product and whatnot. Organizations simply do not metabolize that kind of change that rapidly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. Let me take it one step further. Okay? So I came in six, eight months ago and I was like, 'Holy hell, it is changing up, man. You got check this out.' And you were like, 'Hold on a minute, slow down.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Um that's not how organizations move. And so you and I have essentially not a debate but we've kind of been exploring this the whole time. Now I would say right now we are seeing the counter reaction. And the counter reaction is expressed as none of this stuff is shipping, product is just as hard. You said you were going to get me a lot more code faster and that's great but I actually don't need that much code thanks.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No it's that all that new code's scary, where do I put it? Is it really what I want? Where'd you- like who did you talk to? Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because organizations, people, parts of culture can only have one idea in their head at once, I think we're seeing it go the other direction. I think the AI, the AI companies owned the narrative about six months ago. They're like boom, we did it, we blew up business. Yeah. We blew up government, welcome to our future, please get your wallets out. Okay so that was A. Now I think we're seeing like guys, you didn't do it, I still have to ship all this software, it's a pain in the ass. Yeah. I don't believe that that is the end state either. I think what is actually happening under the hood, 85% of these projects are failing but they're- these- these things are good at parsing natural language, turning it into code, code is really powerful in all sorts of different places. I think that ecologically vibe coding and rapid software development hasn't fully found its niche. People applied it towards legacy kinds of software development and it turns out we just don't need as much software as it can produce. There's a glut.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Not only that, if you look at- think about an organization as this organism that has intent. Right? Um, the IDE didn't revolutionize organizations. They really revolutionized productivity for engineers but it took years for them to say well you know what we should probably standardize tooling but you can frankly use whatever you want if you're talented and you're additive, great, right? And the truth is translating individual productivity into some net outcome at the company level doesn't make any sense. It just doesn't make any sense. Imagine you had 100 great people in a room. Do you know the overhead- when you cross I think 12- when you cross 12 people, the tax of orchestrating work amongst any number from 13 onward-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know my rule: every person added to a group subtracts exactly one IQ point.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So by the time you get to a group like a political- it's a mentally challenged group- by you- when- if- let's- I'll give you an example: the Democrats, probably about 120 million of them, that's a negative 120 million IQ points.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Fair enough. Same with the Republicans. Like it just- the more people you put in, the more it's likely that you have to- eventually you're just saying- you're just leaning into a microphone and saying America. Instead of bitching all this time let's be...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, this is the thing, if anyone would ever listen, it's what I'm saying. Okay, we went from boy it's all blowing up to like eh, hold on, it's a lot of nonsense. I don't think that that's real.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Here's what I think is happening. Engineers have taken control. We've talked a lot about that. Engineers have very specific senses of where code belongs and where it should go.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Fine.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're also not necessarily as much as say like a product manager or designer interested in the holistic function of the business. They're interested in code.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They find it lame.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They do. They don't... and I'm okay with that. It's life. It's just organizations and disciplines have their own cultures.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We are nowhere near the change that is going to happen when access to good tools and software can flow all the way through an organization, especially those with fewer resources. Big NGOs, small NGOs, universities. We are nowhere near what could get unlocked if people were building code for their peers and thinking about in terms of product for their peers. I don't think that happens with our current processes because our current processes are designed for an industrial kind of code production. I think that happens when code almost becomes more like a medium. It's very hard to explain that, but like it's almost like I might assign someone to go off and write a little report on the future of our English department here at this college, right? In the same way they could go off and make some code to help us manage the curriculum.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, look, I have two pieces of advice here and this is kind of overlapping with what you're saying.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But to finish my point, it's so much cheaper to do that specific thing than it used to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. Now and you can be a little more ambitious. I think there are two things you need to do. The first is don't start with the software, start with a goal, right? Number one, meaning, I want to make it easier for doctors to assess contraindications of medicine, right? So let's make it better for them. That's a goal. That's not software, that's an actual goal. Or I want to make it easier to read, you know, EKG.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Now in the past, just stating that meant that you were kind of bananas because software is so hard that you had to focus on getting the software culture in place first before you could get any of its output.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Correct. That's... so start with a goal, not with a solution, not with technology. The second is don't try to conquer the whole continent, pick a hill. Meaning, don't say I want to make sure that doctors are able to get better results across all the tests in these systems and I want them to get it on their phones by end of this year.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The only way you're going to get this is if Claude Code gives you a deadline.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Right. Like you need to be like, alright, can you build me a prototype and you got an hour?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The Josh Tyrangiel book talks about actually a hospital case where they're, you know, sepsis is hard to detect.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're like, if you can get me 30% better predictions, lives will be saved.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Just give me that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's, you know, you walk into a hospital as a patient and you're like, god, they should blow this whole thing up. The whole thing is a—you know, there's Epic and there's all the systems they use. I went in for a procedure years ago and the woman opened up terminal to get me in the system. Like, it was just a green screen. This was like 2018.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Literally that is what AI companies have reduced us to again in terms of vibe check.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We're in terminals again.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So you could always look at the whole thing and you're like, what is that? And the truth is what you're really looking at is years of culture and legacy that are just there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so I can—let me land this for us because I think what's going—here's what I think is happening. I think that—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Stick the landing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, okay, so what happened is everybody came out and said, we're going to reinvent and insert enormous structural thing here: hospitals, science, genetics, cure diseases, business, whatever, okay? Now what you're seeing is people from those institutions going, we tried it, some of it's okay. We're going to have to keep chipping away.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Now and what you're describing and what I'm seeing is a world in which those individual components that comprise the whole system—attacking the whole system rarely works, good luck.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The term I love to use is that whole systems have wicked immune systems.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They really do. They—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: People, their systems, their data, there's all sorts of white blood cells come at you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I used to always talk about a new software product as like a liver transplant and sometimes the body—the body wants to reject it. You'd have to actually figure out ways to do immunosuppression. So now, okay, now let me just—let me close with a little story because I think—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This better be good.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Is it about—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Is it about Palantir?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No. I went and saw somebody talk once, it was a design—it was a design leader, I can't remember her name but she's very, very brilliant and she had worked at an enormous hospital system—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Clearly not that brilliant, you don't remember her name.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: She'd worked at an enormous hospital system just improving the signage.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Very interesting. So classic design problem: like people—we glued this hospital together over 150 years on multiple campuses and nobody knows how to get from oncology to the, you know, to whatever. Elevators, everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: To my lobby signage, elevators.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Everything. But all the way—or you know, you go to the third floor but you need—there's like seven different departments.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is a hot mess. I've been in—yeah, I've seen—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We have two cardiology departments, that kind of thing. So she created like—it was a little card you got, it was wayfinding tool, so on and so forth.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Visual vocabulary, all of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All of it. And at the very end of the talk, somebody said, 'Hey,' and this brilliant person, classic New York like super genius like agency type, and somebody's like—raised their hand and was like, 'Hey, how long did that take?' And she said, 'Seven years.' Okay? I think of that—I think of that every day. Every day I think of that seven years because—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because that is actually what change—Incredible. That's what change looks like at that scale.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Incredible. Yeah, it does.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so what's happened is we have this narrative of absolute, drastic, total structural change. And it's landing at a very disruptive moment where our president is a white-hot ball of lightning who is in there, there's just this sense of—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sizzling ball of lightning.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Whatever though, but there's this sense of institutions and things that felt extremely stable—our climate—it all felt so stable, and now it is starting to really show cracks. And so AI comes into that and is like, 'Guess what else? Guess what else we got?' And so I think that narrative landed and everybody was like, 'Oh man, another thing, another thing.' But the reality is that the institutions are not bending as much as Silicon Valley would like them to.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And yes, and just to put a final note on what you're saying here, I agree with everything you're saying. There is magic in these things. There are ways to use them to make things better.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I was ready. I wanted it to blow up Epic and replace it in two hours. I'm still waiting for that to happen. If they can pull it off, I will step back.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, Epic, if you are listening or watching, somehow my mom's case in a different hospital system is coming into my emails. And that's awkward.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Also Epic. I think it's Mount Sinai and some other...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Wow. So you and your mom are one in Epic account?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I don't know, but I'm having my uterus checked out next week.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hopefully at the doctor's. Onward we go, my friend.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay. Yes, illuminating conversation, Paul.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Inspired by your blog post.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So illuminating. Very nice. John Oates.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: John Oates. Who's the other guy from Wham! who made an album?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, Andrew Ridgeley.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: He made an album called, I think, Son of Arthur, I think is what it's called. And growing up in Bay Ridge, there was a record store on Third Avenue and a friend of mine, every time we walked by it, we'd be like, 'Let's go see if it's still in there.' We always went in to see if the album—if anyone ever bought it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Did it ever sell?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It never sold for years. Record store went out of business. It was like on the way home from dinner we would go into this record store and just make sure it's there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Did you ever listen to it?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No. We're going to go right now. We're going to go to Apple Music and we are going to listen—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What if it's amazing?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It might not be amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It might be the greatest album.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I don't think so.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, Son of Arthur, Andrew Ridgeley. Shout out to—who is the other member of Wham? Many people may not know the other member of Wham.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: George Michael.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There you go. Yeah, I mean, legendary musician, handsome man. That's how we think about software and AI and how to make it successful for you. Reach out at hello@abord.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Anytime. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Have a great week. Bye.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And thanks for bringing up your mom's uterus.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Word.

highlights: While AI tools have made generating code significantly faster and easier, the actual craft of "product"—making software usable, adopted, and valuable—remains a difficult, interdisciplinary grind that technology alone cannot solve.

The narrative of the "one-person unicorn" overlooks the reality that successful software requires a rare combination of design, technical, and product management skills that few individuals possess simultaneously.

Large organizations possess "wicked immune systems" made of calcified cultures and complex regulations that prevent them from metabolizing rapid technological change as quickly as Silicon Valley expects.

To find success with AI, leaders should focus on specific, achievable goals and "pick a hill" to conquer rather than attempting to overhaul entire systemic infrastructures or legacy systems all at once.

Software is fundamentally a social and cultural artifact that requires user engagement and institutional humility to succeed, meaning adoption is a far more critical metric than the volume of code produced.
summary:
In this episode of the *Aboard* podcast, hosts Paul Ford and Rich Ziade discuss the growing disconnect between Silicon Valley’s hyper-optimistic AI narrative and the practical realities of the "real world." A central theme of the conversation is the distinction between "coding" and "product." While AI tools like Claude have made writing code significantly faster—a phenomenon they refer to as "vibe coding"—the hosts argue that the "product grind" (the difficult work of design, user empathy, and interdisciplinary collaboration) remains as challenging as ever. They suggest that while the Valley focuses on the potential for one-person unicorns, truly transformative software still requires a deep understanding of human and cultural contexts.

The discussion also highlights the "wicked immune systems" of large organizations, such as hospitals and government agencies, which often reject rapid technological disruption. The hosts critique the tech industry’s tendency to use "extinction rhetoric" to pressure institutions into adopting AI, noting that these calcified cultures require patient diplomacy rather than just more code. They conclude that for AI to be truly effective in the enterprise sector, developers must move away from trying to overhaul entire systems overnight. Instead, they should focus on specific, high-value goals—or "picking a hill"—where AI can solve a discrete problem, acknowledging that meaningful institutional change often takes years of steady, incremental progress.
description:
Silicon Valley “disruption” is at odds with the way software creation actually works—and that’s a problem. On this week’s podcast, Paul and Rich discuss Rich’s recent Aboard newsletter installment, “Maybe the Valley Could Listen a Little.” about how the dream of an “AI unicorn” who can use these tools to build a product all by themselves is a total fantasy. AI companies are promising revolutionary transformation across industries, but are they mostly just plastering over fundamental issues in processes and systems?

Captions are auto-generated; for audio, show notes, and a full transcript, visit our website: https://aboard.com/podcast/ai-unicorns-dont-exist/
topics: The disconnect between Silicon Valley's culture of disruption and the reality of large organizations
The myth of the one-person AI unicorn and the difficulty of product management
How AI simplifies code generation but fails to simplify the complexities of software design and adoption
The challenges of implementing AI in calcified institutions like healthcare and government
The importance of starting with specific business goals rather than technology solutions

youtube_metadata.title: AI Unicorns Don’t Exist | The Aboard Podcast 2026-07-14

On this week’s podcast, Paul and Rich discuss Rich’s recent Aboard newsletter installment, “Maybe the Valley Could Listen a Little.” about how the dream of an “AI unicorn” who can use these tools to build a product all by themselves is a total fantasy. AI companies are promising revolutionary transformation across industries, but are they mostly just plastering over fundamental issues in processes and systems?
